ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for plain waffle; not a clinically coded food item in standard SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium plain waffle weighing approximately 75 g, served without toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Waffle, plain, frozen, ready-to-heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 18370); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry F11100033
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo record F11100033 (confidence 0.95, reviewed). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain waffle (NDB 18370), which reports ~3.8 g saturated fat per 100 g for a comparable plain waffle made with pancake batter. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrients beyond calcium and iron are set to -1 where not reported by HPB; USDA SR Legacy values are used as secondary reference for those fields where available.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for waffle (plain, homemade)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 76 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain waffle (homemade, wheat flour base), consistent with the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Am J Clin Nutr 2008;87:223S–236S). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 50.4 × 0.75) / 100 = 28.7 for a 75 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (17.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure; sodium at 127 mg/100 g is moderate.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit plain waffle consumption due to its high sugar load (17.2 g/100 g), which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises cardiovascular risk independent of its moderate sodium content (127 mg/100 g); if consumed, pair with unsweetened protein sources and avoid syrup or cream toppings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (76), high sugar (17.2 g/100 g), high carbohydrate (50.4 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) combine to produce a high glycemic load (GL ~28.7 per 75 g serving), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain waffles are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to a high GI of 76, sugar content of 17.2 g/100 g exceeding the 15 g threshold, and dietary fibre of only 1.8 g/100 g; consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to small portions paired with high-fibre or protein-rich foods to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), high sugar (17.2 g/100 g), and cholesterol (~52 mg/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume plain waffles only occasionally, as the combination of saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and high sugar (17.2 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol and triglycerides; substituting butter with unsaturated oils and reducing added sugar would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from butter, combined with high sugar (17.2 g/100 g), promote pro-inflammatory mediators and are associated with increased disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid plain waffles due to their pro-inflammatory profile: saturated fat ~3.8 g/100 g, trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g, and sugar 17.2 g/100 g all promote inflammatory cytokine production; substituting butter with extra-virgin olive oil and reducing sugar would substantially lower the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A plain waffle is a grid-patterned baked snack made from a batter similar to pancake batter, typically containing wheat flour, eggs, milk, butter, sugar, and baking powder. The batter is poured into a preheated waffle iron and cooked until golden and crisp on the outside with a soft, slightly chewy interior. Plain waffles are widely consumed as a breakfast item or snack in Western countries and are popular in Singapore as a street snack, often sold plain or with sweet toppings. Per 100 g, a plain waffle provides 371 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 15.9 g fat, 50.4 g carbohydrate, 17.2 g sugar, and only 1.8 g dietary fibre. The high sugar content (17.2 g/100 g) combined with a high glycemic index of 76 and low fibre makes plain waffles a high-glycemic food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. This profile makes them unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood sugar. The saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from butter also raise cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns. Clinically, plain waffles are a high-energy, high-sugar, low-fibre processed snack that should be consumed in moderation by the general population and avoided or strictly limited by individuals with diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plain waffle?
Is waffle good for weight loss?
Can I eat waffle if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of waffle?
Is waffle suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record F11100033 with a stated confidence of 0.95 and review status 'reviewed'. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, water, and B-vitamins are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a comparable plain waffle. GI of 76 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is rated high.
notes: Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (4.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (6.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), cholesterol (52 mg/100 g), potassium (110 mg/100 g), phosphorus (170 mg/100 g), magnesium (12 mg/100 g), zinc (0.5 mg/100 g), water (26 g/100 g), thiamine (0.18 mg/100 g), riboflavin (0.19 mg/100 g), and niacin (1.5 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (plain waffle, NDB 18370) as these fields are not reported in the HPB source record. Fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy for this food category with sufficient specificity to assign a value; these remain as -1 pending a more detailed compositional analysis. No zero-value core macros are present. This food is Western/International in origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual rule, which applies to Singaporean dishes. The food_name 'Waffle (plain)' is the canonical English name as used in the HPB source. Trans fat value of 0.1 g/100 g is an estimate from USDA SR Legacy and is consistent with the input health_suitability caution noting trans fat presence.

Set vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg to -1 with documented justification in notes.
